quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Squeeze Theorem"?

options.text: If g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ h(x) near c and lim g = lim h = L, then lim f = L; also called the Sandwich Theorem
Speeding up: v(t) and a(t) have the same sign; slowing down: they have opposite signs
A relation where y is defined implicitly by an equation F(x,y) = 0 rather than explicitly as y = f(x)
Change of base: logₐ x = ln x / ln a, so d/dx[logₐ x] = 1/(x ln a)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Squeeze Theorem" is defined as: If g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ h(x) near c and lim g = lim h = L, then lim f = L; also called the Sandwich Theorem. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Special trigonometric limits"?

options.text: Vertical: where denominator = 0 (numerator ≠ 0); horizontal: limits at ±∞; oblique: when degree(num) = degree(den) + 1
All variables are functions of time, so d/dt[x²] = 2x·dx/dt, d/dt[xy] = x·dy/dt + y·dx/dt
d/dx[f(x) ± g(x)] = f'(x) ± g'(x); derivative of a sum/difference is the sum/difference of derivatives
lim(θ→0) sin(θ)/θ = 1 and lim(θ→0) (1-cos(θ))/θ = 0; foundational for deriving trig derivatives

quiz_questions.explanation: "Special trigonometric limits" is defined as: lim(θ→0) sin(θ)/θ = 1 and lim(θ→0) (1-cos(θ))/θ = 0; foundational for deriving trig derivatives. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Horizontal asymptote"?

options.text: If f' changes from + to - at c: local maximum; from - to +: local minimum; no sign change: neither
Sometimes algebraic simplification (canceling, expanding, dividing) before differentiation is easier than applying rules to complex expressions
A horizontal line y = L that the graph approaches as x → ±∞; exists when lim(x→±∞) f(x) = L
P = K: if the population is perturbed slightly above or below K, it returns to K

quiz_questions.explanation: "Horizontal asymptote" is defined as: A horizontal line y = L that the graph approaches as x → ±∞; exists when lim(x→±∞) f(x) = L. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "End behavior"?

options.text: Change of base: logₐ x = ln x / ln a, so d/dx[logₐ x] = 1/(x ln a)
Divide the plate into thin horizontal strips of width dh; each strip has approximately constant pressure ρgh and area w(h)·dh
The behavior of f(x) as x → +∞ or x → -∞; determined by the dominant (highest-degree) terms
Two functions are asymptotically equivalent if lim(x→∞) f(x)/g(x) = 1

quiz_questions.explanation: "End behavior" is defined as: The behavior of f(x) as x → +∞ or x → -∞; determined by the dominant (highest-degree) terms. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Limits at infinity for rationals"?

options.text: EVT guarantees extrema exist but does not tell us where; we use critical point analysis and endpoint evaluation to locate them
Local min: concave up valley (f'' > 0); local max: concave down hill (f'' < 0)
Numerical approximation: start at (x₀,y₀), step by yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + f(xₙ,yₙ)·Δx with step size Δx
deg(num)  deg(den): limit = ±∞

quiz_questions.explanation: "Limits at infinity for rationals" is defined as: deg(num)  deg(den): limit = ±∞. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "If g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ h(x) near c and lim g = lim h = L, then lim f = L; also called the Sandwich Theorem"?

options.text: Position, velocity, acceleration
Direct substitution

options.text: For rational functions, compare the degrees of numerator and denominator: same degree → ratio of leading coefficients.
Riemann sums approximate this area using rectangles: left, right, and midpoint sums with n subintervals.
The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus connects differentiation and integration: d/dx[∫ₐˣ f(t)dt] = f(x).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of The Squeeze Theorem and Limits at Infinity?

options.text: Financial mathematics: differentiating revenue functions R(x) to find marginal revenue and determine the optimal quantity to maximize profit
Average voltage of AC power: V_avg = (1/T)∫₀ᵀ V(t)dt; for sinusoidal AC, the average over a full cycle is zero, motivating the use of RMS voltage
Proving that a polynomial of degree n has at most n-1 critical points: applying Rolle's Theorem between consecutive roots of f
The Squeeze Theorem proving that oscillating functions with decreasing amplitude converge: lim(x→∞) sin(x)/x = 0 since -1/x ≤ sin(x)/x ≤ 1/x

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of The Squeeze Theorem and Limits at Infinity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of The Squeeze Theorem and Limits at Infinity?

options.text: Aquarium design: the total force on viewing windows increases dramatically with depth, requiring thicker glass for deeper tanks
Finding the slope of a circle x² + y² = 25 at any point: dy/dx = -x/y, giving the tangent line direction for navigation and engineering applications
Long-term drug concentration models: horizontal asymptotes represent the steady-state drug level that the body approaches with regular dosing
Structural load analysis: EVT guarantees the existence of a maximum stress point on a continuous beam, ensuring engineers can find and design for worst-case loads

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of The Squeeze Theorem and Limits at Infinity.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is The Squeeze Theorem and Limits at Infinity applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Mountain hiking: the highest point on a trail (absolute max) and the lowest point (absolute min) are found by checking peaks, valleys, and the trail endpoints
Economics: long-run average cost curves approaching horizontal asymptotes representing minimum efficient scale of production
Estimating total rainfall from rate-of-rainfall data: ∫(rainfall rate)dt gives total accumulated precipitation over a storm event
Engineering error analysis: propagation of measurement uncertainty through formulas using differentials to estimate how input errors affect computed results

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of The Squeeze Theorem and Limits at Infinity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of The Squeeze Theorem and Limits at Infinity?

options.text: Radioactive decay: integrating the decay rate dN/dt = -λN gives N(t) = N₀e^(-λt), the fundamental decay equation
Modeling the trajectory of a projectile with air resistance: the implicit equation relating position components requires combined chain rule and implicit differentiation
Profit maximization: after finding the critical point of P(x), checking P''(x) < 0 confirms it's a maximum profit level, not a minimum
The Squeeze Theorem proving that oscillating functions with decreasing amplitude converge: lim(x→∞) sin(x)/x = 0 since -1/x ≤ sin(x)/x ≤ 1/x

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of The Squeeze Theorem and Limits at Infinity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows The Squeeze Theorem and Limits at Infinity in practice?

options.text: Planetary orbits: elliptical orbits x²/a² + y²/b² = 1 analyzed with implicit differentiation to find orbital velocity direction at each point
AP Calculus free-response questions consistently test the ability to set up integrals from verbal descriptions and geometric reasoning
Long-term drug concentration models: horizontal asymptotes represent the steady-state drug level that the body approaches with regular dosing
Speedometer readings as instantaneous velocity: the derivative of position with respect to time at each moment, computed electronically in modern vehicles

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of The Squeeze Theorem and Limits at Infinity.
